nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found in rocky and coral reef habitats at depths of approximately 5–25 m. Has been documented sheltering in caves and crevices within reef structures.

decorPreferences: caves and crevices sized to conceal the entire body
stable live rock structure (secure rockwork to prevent collapses)
tight-fitting, sealed lid with no gaps

diet.notes: Primarily a fish and crustacean predator. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen or freeze-dried krill, whole fish, silver sides, chopped squid, mysis shrimp, and other meaty marine foods. Newly acquired individuals may initially require live ghost shrimp or live feeder fish to trigger a feeding response; most eventually transition to prepared foods. Feeding with tongs or a feeding stick is generally advised, as the species has relatively poor eyesight and has been known to bite hands. Juveniles are typically fed twice weekly; adults once weekly tends to be sufficient.

goodWith: large, robust fish not small enough to be swallowed
avoidWith: all crustaceans — shrimp, crabs, lobsters, hermit crabs (natural prey)
other eels unless tank is very large (150+ gal / 570+ L) and both introduced simultaneously

imageCandidates.notes: LASZLO ILYES; 4368x2912. Tack-sharp, well-lit head emerging from reef; classic jeweled look with dense warm gold rosette spots on dark reticulated body, mouth agape. Best diagnostic and most striking.
Diego Delso; 5313x3542. Sharp side profile showing more of the body length, yellow-centred dark spots clearly resolved along the flank; complements the head close-up.
Diego Delso; 3698x2465. Sharp in-situ head shot of a different individual, gold rosette pattern well shown; slight green water cast.

summary: The Jeweled Moray Eel (Muraena lentiginosa) is an Eastern Pacific reef predator known for its striking coloration — juveniles display brown with gold spots, while adults develop a black body with white markings. It is generally considered hardy, though it is also aggressive and highly prone to escaping.
careNotes:
The Jeweled Moray Eel is commonly considered a hardy species that adapts well to aquarium conditions, but its aggressive disposition, large adult size, and strong escape tendencies make it better suited to experienced keepers rather than beginners.

A tightly sealed aquarium lid — weighted or mechanically secured — is considered essential; this species has been widely documented escaping through any gap. Efficient biological filtration and a protein skimmer are generally advised given the eel's heavy bioload.

It is generally not considered reef-safe: the species will prey on any crustacean in the tank (shrimp, hermit crabs, crabs, lobsters) and can accidentally damage invertebrates due to its activity.

Juveniles display brown coloration with gold spots; adults transition to a black body with white or light-colored markings. This color phase change can cause juveniles to be misidentified or sold separately in the trade.

Conspecific housing has been known to work in tanks of roughly 150 gallons (570 L) or more when both individuals are introduced simultaneously, though aggression between individuals has been reported.
breedingNotes: Captive reproduction is generally considered not achieved under typical home aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page: care level easy, temperament aggressive, reef safe no, 50 gal minimum, 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, adult size 24 in.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked: care level beginner, temperament aggressive, reef safe no, 50 gal minimum, adult size 24 in, carnivore. Water parameters not stated. Confirmed tight-lid escape risk.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked: care level easy, temperament aggressive, 90 gal minimum (largest recommendation), 72–80°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4. Conspecific minimum 150 gal. Nocturnal behavior confirmed. Cave decor needs detailed.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Cross-checked: adult size 24 in, temperature 76–80°F, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, dKH 8–12, family Muraenidae. Native range confirmed Eastern Pacific, Gulf of California to Central America. Juvenile/adult color phase difference documented.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked: care level moderate (only source rating it moderate vs easy on others), temperament aggressive, reef safe no, 75 gal minimum, adult size 24 in. Juvenile brown-with-gold-spots color phase confirmed.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented in reviewed sources.
